critique.summary: The article reports that Honda is discontinuing its electric vehicle (EV) programs, including halting development of new EV models and stopping production of the Prologue, a GM-built EV. The author argues this decision risks Honda falling behind in the automotive industry's shift toward electric drivetrains and software-defined vehicles, potentially jeopardizing its future competitiveness.
mainPoints: Honda has stopped development of its electric Acura RDX, Honda 0 sedan and SUV, and will cease production of the GM-designed Prologue EV.
The company blames U.S. tariffs and Chinese competition but lacked a strong EV strategy from the start.
Honda risks missing out on key industry shifts toward electric drivetrains and software-defined vehicles (SDVs).
Legacy automakers like Honda often struggle with EVs because they try to adapt internal combustion engine platforms rather than design EVs from the ground up.
Honda's identity as an internal combustion engine and driver-focused car company is challenged by the rise of EVs and autonomous driving technologies.
Honda's competitiveness is declining, especially in China, where it faces losses and strong competition from newer EV manufacturers.
critique.truthfulness: The article's claims align with publicly available information as of early 2026, including reports from Automotive News about Honda halting EV projects and discontinuing the Prologue. The challenges faced by legacy automakers in transitioning to EVs and software-defined vehicles are widely documented in industry analysis. Honda's financial struggles and competitive pressures in China are also supported by recent earnings reports. However, the article presents a critical perspective without citing multiple sources or perspectives, relying heavily on analysis and opinion from the author.
considerations: The article does not provide direct statements or responses from Honda beyond referencing their earnings report.
It lacks perspectives from industry analysts, Honda executives, or other stakeholders that might contextualize Honda's strategic decisions.
The financial and strategic implications for Honda's global operations beyond China are not deeply explored.
Potential plans Honda may have for future EV or SDV development beyond the current halted projects are not discussed.
The article does not consider the broader market or regulatory factors influencing Honda's decisions in detail.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on Honda's decision to halt its EV development, with commenters debating the implications for Honda and the broader automotive industry. Key themes include skepticism about software-defined vehicles, concerns about EV infrastructure and practicality, and comparisons between legacy automakers and emerging EV leaders.

comments.author: TrackerFF
comments.text:
I live in a top EV market, Norway.

ICE cars have been planned out for years now, and something like 96% of all new cars in Norway were EV last year.

Basically, if you plan on keeping selling ICE cars, you're removing yourself from the market here. There's no future for new personal ICE cars here.

I figure most other countries will be the same.
comments.analysis: This comment provides accurate, data-backed insight into EV adoption in Norway, a leading EV market, supporting the argument that ICE vehicles are losing market share.

comments.author: Denatonium
comments.text: Calling the Prologue "Honda's EV" feels like a huge stretch. The Prologue was a rebadged GM vehicle that served strictly as a compliance car for meeting CAFE standards. Now that the CAFE standards have been rendered toothless, there's no longer a need for that deal.
comments.analysis: This comment correctly identifies the Prologue as a rebadged GM vehicle, highlighting the limited nature of Honda's EV efforts and the regulatory motivations behind it.

comments.author: rkagerer
comments.text:
Honda is setting itself up for failure on the second disruption sweeping the automotive industry: the software-defined vehicle (SDV), which has core capabilities that can be upgraded and improved over time.

No thank you. Not sure why the author frames this as a good thing. They've been bamboozled by the automakers and have got it backwards - you're buying a vehicle that already has the capabilities, but are disabled, then paying rent (or a fee) to turn them on. I'm much more likely to buy from a manufacturer that doesn't play these games.
comments.analysis: The comment insightfully critiques Honda's strategy in the context of software-defined vehicles, emphasizing the importance of software upgrades in future automotive competitiveness.

comments.author: pclowes
comments.text:
Everyone is saying EVs are the future but most EVs cannot compete with many of Honda’s offerings.

Eg. I need to move 6 people and significant gear (skiing, camping, biking etc) long remote distances.

There is no EV that can do that really. And the ones that come close are easily $20-30k higher than an Odyssey. Plus the durability of large EVs is far from proven while the 300k mile club of Odyssey owners is large.

I need Suburban/Minivan functionality out of a proven OEM at a competitive price point. (I also need to see my friends with Rivians etc not having to schedule their vacation around charger availability. Have seen this waste hours and hours of time)
comments.analysis: This comment raises valid concerns about current EV limitations in capacity, range, and cost compared to traditional vehicles, reflecting practical consumer considerations.

comments.author: billfor
comments.text:
"Here, Honda is setting itself up for failure on the second disruption sweeping the automotive industry: the software-defined vehicle (SDV), which has core capabilities that can be upgraded and improved over time."

I'll pay triple for a non software defined vehicle that doesn't track me and can't be touched by the dealer once I purchase it. My one SDV (Tesla) is still on FSD from 2023 because the newer versions are terrible judging from the comments on the Tesla forums.
comments.analysis: Expresses a consumer perspective skeptical of software-defined vehicles and privacy concerns, reflecting a valid segment of the market wary of over-the-air controls.

comments.author: kleiba
comments.text:
> Consumers, mostly those who buy EVs from the likes of Tesla, Rivian, and BYD, have grown accustomed to the frequent updates, slick infotainment software, and advanced driver-assistance systems.

Guess which three items out of that list I do not want.
comments.analysis: Highlights a counterpoint to the tech enthusiasm around EVs, emphasizing that not all consumers desire constant software updates or advanced features.

comments.author: jedberg
comments.text:
This is so unfortunate. I was never a van guy, but my wife insisted we get a van, so I got the Honda. And honestly? I kinda love it. It drives like a car but holds eight people (or four people and a whole bunch of luggage).

The way we use the van, 90% of our drives are under 20 miles round trip. The rest are longer road trips. I've been waiting eight years for Honda to make an electric or even a plug-in hybrid where the gas motor just charge the battery.

It would be perfect for my family. I guess that's not happening now.
comments.analysis: Reflects consumer disappointment with Honda's EV strategy, particularly in the minivan segment, underscoring gaps in product offerings.

comments.author: small_model
comments.text: All the legacy automakers that haven't fully moved to EV's PROFITABLY will go defacto bankrupt within a few years, there will be some mergers to stay alive but it's game over. Tesla and China companies will own auto, with Tesla capturing most the profit, similar to Apple vs Android phones. Autonomy will further accelerate this.
comments.analysis: While somewhat speculative, this comment correctly identifies the financial risks legacy automakers face if they fail to transition profitably to EVs.

comments.author: nonford150
comments.text:
I almost pulled the trigger on a Prologue; so glad I had second thoughts. Even though it was essentially a GM product, I've only ever owned Hondas, so I thought "Well, at least I can get service at my Honda dealer".

Charging in the US (other than at home) is still the biggest issue for me. I do lots of traveling, and waiting 30-45 minutes to charge even at a Level 3 charger is a PITA. If I had a J std charger, then it's even longer. This makes my monthly 8 hour trips one-way another 2 hours - this sucks. Sorry - I'll keep my 2005 Honda Element with 445K miles. Another engine would be cheaper than less than a year of car payments. And it's pretty much indestructible.
comments.analysis: Accurately points out a major practical barrier to EV adoption in the US: charging infrastructure and time, especially for long-distance travel.

comments.author: GianFabien
comments.text:
Smart doorbells and thermostats that upgraded in the night often became a nuisance or an expensive brick. But a faulty software upgrade on a car can kill you and others.

Car company execs need to take a chill pill followed by a reality serum. Monetizing subscription based basic features and delivering in-car advertising is the absolutely worst way to go.

As consumers we need to stop buying into the bells, whistles and trinkets and demand essential and safe transportation.
comments.analysis: Raises valid safety concerns about software updates in vehicles, emphasizing the higher stakes compared to consumer electronics.
